Tall. Walked upright but wrong, like the joints bent backward.
It left marks on the stones. Deep gouges, evenly spaced.
Bigger than a horse, from what they said. And fast.
The fur was matted and dark, almost oily. Stank like a week-old kill.
No eyes that anyone could see. Just a flat, pale face and a mouth that opened too wide.
It didn't run so much as glide. Feet barely touching the ground.
Three toes on each print. Deep in the front, like it leans forward when it moves.
Something big. Moved like it knew where it was going.
The teeth marks don't match anything the hunters know. Too wide apart, too clean.
Moved in a pack of four or five. Coordinated, like wolves, but silent.
Whatever it was, it could climb. The claw marks went straight up the cliff face.
Twice the size of a man at the shoulder. Covered in something between fur and quills.
It circled the camp three times before it left. Testing us, I think.
The tracks show it was dragging something heavy. Then the drag marks just stop.
Long limbs, thin. Like someone stretched a person out and taught it to crawl.
A farmer saw it feeding at dawn. Said it pulled the carcass apart like wet bread.
It doesn't leave waste behind. Eats everything: bone, hide, hooves. Nothing left.
Fast. Whatever it was, it covered ground like nothing I've seen.
One of the scouts found a nest. Woven from branches and sinew, big enough to sleep in.
It screamed once. Not like an animal. More like metal dragging across stone.
The old hunter says it matches stories his grandmother told. Something that comes when the season turns.
Its blood, or whatever it left behind, burned the grass where it dripped.
Walked on all fours mostly, but stood up when it spotted the lantern light.
It made a sound when it moved. Low, like something heavy being dragged through brush.
It watched the village for two nights before it took anything. Patient creature, whatever it is.
The hide was thick enough that an arrow bounced off. Not deflected: bounced, like hitting a wall.
It had a ridge of bone running down its back. Like armor growing out of the spine.
The thing shed scales wherever it went. Thin, translucent, almost like glass shards.
It moved in total darkness without hesitation. Whatever senses it uses, they are not eyes.
The paw prints were warm to the touch an hour after it passed. Something about the creature throws heat.
Smelled of sulfur. Not faintly: the kind of stench that gets into your clothes and stays for days.
It had six legs. The front pair were smaller, tucked close to the chest. Used them for gripping.
The tail was barbed. Not at the tip: along the whole underside, like a rasp.
Two sets of jaws. The outer set held the prey while the inner set did the actual damage.
Its eyes reflected light at night, but the wrong color. Green-gold, like nothing in the local woods.
The wingspan was wider than a wagon. The farmer only saw it because it blotted out the moon.
It tunneled. Found the burrow entrance next morning: perfectly round, smooth-walled, going straight down.
The saliva corroded metal. A hunter's knife started pitting within minutes of contact.
Its call carries for miles. Low, resonant, almost below what you can hear. You feel it in your chest.
Covered in something wet and reflective. Not slime exactly: more like a thin layer of oil that caught the light.
The hooves were split into three sections, each moving independently. That's how it grips the rock face.
Short, dense fur that changed color depending on the angle. Brown from the front, almost silver from the side.
Hands. It had hands. Not paws shaped like hands: actual gripping digits with joints in the right places.
One horn, centered, curved back along the skull. Scored with deep grooves like old wood.
The teeth were flat at the front and serrated at the back. Built for tearing, not chewing.
It left behind a musky scent that lingered for days. The dogs refused to track past a certain point.
The prints show it was pacing. Back and forth along the same line, like it was waiting for something.
A row of spines ran down each foreleg. They clattered when the creature moved, like dry reeds.
It wasn't eating the livestock. It was collecting them. Herding the bodies into a pile.
The claws retracted. You could see the tracks change from deep gouges to flat pads when it slowed down.
It had no visible ears. Flat head, smooth on the sides. It tracked sound by turning its whole body.
Something about its gait was wrong. Too smooth. Animals lurch and shift weight: this thing flowed.
The feathers were black but iridescent. Caught the firelight in deep purple and green.
When it opened its mouth, there was a second membrane behind the teeth. Thin, almost translucent.
The body was covered in scars. Old ones, healed over. This thing has been fighting for a long time.
It dragged its belly on the ground when it moved slow. Lifted up high when it charged.
The tongue was forked and longer than my forearm. It tasted the air constantly, flicking side to side.
Built low and heavy. Shoulders like a bull but the neck was too long, too flexible.
When it stood still, it was almost invisible against the stone. The skin matched perfectly.
It bled dark. Not red: a deep brown-black that dried into a crust within seconds.
The eggs were the size of a fist. Hard-shelled, speckled, warm even in cold weather.
The creature left claw marks on the wooden beams ten feet off the ground. It was reaching, not climbing.
Bioluminescent spots along its flanks. Faint blue-green, pulsing slowly like breathing.
It shed its skin in one piece. Found the husk hanging from a tree branch, split down the back.
When threatened, it inflated. The whole torso expanded outward like a bladder, doubling its apparent size.
The mandibles could snap a branch as thick as my wrist. Clean through, one bite.
Something was growing on it. Fungal-looking, pale, clustered along the shoulders and spine.
It walked backward as easily as forward. Same speed, same control. The scouts found that unsettling.
The webbing between its digits let it glide short distances. Not true flight, but enough to close ground fast.
When it died, the body deflated. Whatever was inside weighed a fraction of what the living creature did.
Its breath fogged the air even on a warm night. Cold-blooded in the truest sense.
Armored plates overlapping like roof tiles. Arrows stuck in the gaps but didn't reach the body underneath.
The whiskers were rigid, almost like antennae. They twitched independently, mapping the space around it.
It hunts by ambush. Stays motionless for hours, then strikes in a single lunge. No chase.
We found scratch marks on the inside of a hollow tree. Like it was using the space as a den and marking its walls.
Its footsteps left shallow depressions that filled with a foul-smelling liquid. Seeped right out of the ground.
The thing could flatten itself. Squeezed through a gap in the fence no wider than my hand.
The skull was elongated, narrow. Built for pushing through dense undergrowth without snagging.
Every kill site was the same: the bones were arranged neatly. Not scattered. Placed.
The wings were vestigial. Too small for flight but it used them for balance when it leaped.
It vocalizes before a kill. A short, sharp bark. The hunters learned to dread that sound.
The claws were curved inward, like hooks. Made for pulling things toward it, not slashing outward.
It moved through water without a ripple. Submerged completely, only the nostrils above the surface.
The creature was blind in daylight. Stayed underground until dark, then moved with perfect confidence.
We found quills embedded in a tree trunk. Launched from the body, not shed. Some kind of defense mechanism.
Its jaw unhinged. Watched it swallow something twice the width of its own head.
The footprints were asymmetrical. One side deeper than the other, like it favored a limb. Old injury, maybe.
The teeth regrow. Found a pile of shed fangs near the den entrance, ranging from fresh to worn down.
It dug a network of shallow trenches around the area. Territorial markings, or possibly drainage for its den.
A pair of them hunted together. One drove the prey while the other waited at the bottleneck. Planned it.
The creature secreted a resin that hardened on contact with air. Used it to seal its burrow entrance.
Thin, membrane-like wings stretched between elongated fingers. Bat-like, but the proportions were all wrong.
It mimicked sounds. The patrol heard what they thought was a bird call, but it came from ground level.
The thing left marks on trees: not claw marks, but rubbing marks, like a bear, only much higher up.
The bones in the kill were cracked lengthwise, not across. It was after the marrow specifically.
Its coloring shifted with the season. Summer sightings describe a tawny brown; winter reports say pale grey.
The nest was built around a heat source. Hot rocks, maybe a thermal vent. The eggs need warmth to develop.
It had what looked like a second pair of eyes set further back on the skull. Whether they functioned is anyone's guess.
The spoor was dry and powdery. Full of bone fragments and compacted fur. A strict carnivore.
It stacked stones. Small cairns, three or four rocks high, placed along the perimeter of its territory.
The growl was subsonic. You couldn't hear it so much as feel your teeth vibrate.
It swam upstream against a strong current without apparent effort. The body was built for water.
The creature ignored the livestock entirely and went straight for the grain stores. Herbivore, but a destructive one.
Its hide secreted something caustic. The rope we tried to bind it with frayed apart within the hour.
The tracks indicate it walks on the knuckles of its forelimbs, not the pads. Unusual gait.
Something had parasites on it. Smaller creatures riding on the back, picking at the hide. Symbiotic, maybe.
The burrow went down at a steep angle, then leveled off. Could hear water running at the bottom.
When cornered, it flared a crest of cartilage along its neck. Bright red underneath. Warning display.
It left a trail of disturbed earth. Not footprints: the whole path was churned, as if something heavy slithered.
The body was segmented. Flexible like a centipede but with a vertebrate head and jaws.
It only fed at twilight. Dawn and dusk, fifteen minutes each. The rest of the time it was perfectly still.
We found half a dozen caches buried around the territory. It hoards food like a squirrel but on a much larger scale.
The antlers were shed and regrowing. Velvet-covered, swollen with blood vessels. Tender, probably. Didn't stop it from charging.
It communicated with others of its kind through ground vibrations. You could feel the thumping through the soles of your boots.
The creature was old. You could tell by the layers of scarring and the way its movements had slowed. Still dangerous.
Its venom didn't kill outright. The victims reported numbness spreading from the bite, then paralysis. Took hours.
The maw was lined with backward-pointing barbs. Anything that went in was not coming back out.
It built a dam. Not like a beaver: cruder, larger, using whole tree trunks. Flooded the low field.
The song it made at night was almost beautiful. Three rising notes, a pause, then a descending trill. Eerie.
Mucus coated everything it touched. Thick, viscous, and it hardened into a tacky residue by morning.
It avoided fire but was not afraid of it. Kept its distance without panic, circling just outside the light.
The hatchlings were already venomous. Found that out the hard way.
It used tools. A flat stone to crack open shells. Held it between two digits and struck repeatedly.
The dorsal fin was tall enough to spot above the waterline from a distance. Serrated edge, not smooth.
It cached the inedible parts of its kills: bones, hooves, antlers. Piled them near the den entrance like trophies.
The thing could hold its breath for an extraordinary time. Waited submerged in a pond for the better part of an afternoon.
When provoked, it rattled. Dry, rapid clicking from somewhere in the throat. Like a warning rattle but deeper.
Its footprint showed an extra digit. Dewclaw, maybe, but fully functional. Five toes where four should be.
The feathers were barbed. Handling them left fine cuts on the skin, like paper edges.
It constructed a bower. Decorated with bright objects: river stones, bits of glass, a brass buckle from someone's harness.
The creature was territorial about the water source. Anything that approached the stream within fifty paces was charged.
Its exoskeleton was layered. The outer shell was hard, but beneath it was a softer, flexible layer that absorbed impacts.
The herd moved in a spiral pattern. Round and round the grazing area, trampling the center flat.
It climbed by driving its claws into the bark and pulling itself up. Left deep parallel scars on every tree it touched.
Found pellets of compacted bone and hair beneath its roosting site. Owl-like digestion but much larger prey.
The scales were loose, almost like chain mail. They shifted and rattled when the creature moved, creating a dry whisper.
Its tail was prehensile. Used it to anchor itself to branches while it fed, hanging inverted.
The creature dug false tunnels. Dead ends that collapsed when weight was applied. Defensive architecture.
It sprayed a jet of liquid when startled. Hit a tree trunk from ten paces and stripped the bark clean off.
The mating call was two-toned. A high whistle overlaid with a low drone, produced simultaneously. Unsettling.
The juvenile was roughly the size of a large dog. If that is the young, I do not want to see the adult.
It slept standing up, locked in place by specialized joints that braced when the muscles relaxed.
When killed, the flesh began to decompose within minutes. Abnormally fast. Something about its biology runs hot.
The creature had been tagged. An old mark, faded, cut into the ear. Someone had studied it before.
It marked trees with a chemical scent so strong it made your eyes water from a distance.
The fangs were hollow. Envenomation, most likely, though nobody has survived a bite long enough to confirm the mechanism.
The foot pads were calloused and thick. It crossed terrain that would shred a horse's hooves without slowing.
It moved through the canopy as easily as the ground. Leaped between branches with unsettling precision.
Something about its posture changed when it noticed us. Straightened up, lowered its head, went very still.
The kill was surgical. One bite to the neck, no other marks on the body. Clean, practiced, efficient.
The cocoon was spun from a silk-like material. Tough: a blade barely cut through it, and it resisted fire.
It gave a distress call and three more answered from different directions. Surrounded before anyone realized it.
The horns were asymmetrical. One curved up, the other grew straight forward. Battle damage, maybe, or just the species.
The skin was translucent in patches. You could see the musculature working underneath. Deeply unpleasant.
It ate earth. Whole mouthfuls of soil, deliberately, like it was extracting something from the minerals.
The tracks overlapped in a way that suggests it paces the same route nightly. Obsessive, almost ritualistic.
It produced silk from glands behind its jaws. Not fine thread: thick ropes of the stuff, strong enough to bear weight.
We found feathers in the kill zone, but the prey was a mammal. The creature sheds during a kill from exertion.
Its underbelly was soft and pale. That is the vulnerable point, but getting to it means getting underneath the thing.
The creature was nocturnal by preference but not by necessity. On overcast days, it hunted through the afternoon.
It left its young in shallow scrapes in the ground, covered with debris. Difficult to spot until you are standing on one.
The thing could mimic the sound of running water. Drew prey toward it by imitating a stream.
Bony protrusions along the brow formed a natural visor. Shaded the eyes, protected them in a charge.
The creature's weight cracked flagstones when it crossed the courtyard. Heavy beyond what its size suggests.
When still, it was indistinguishable from a boulder. The texture and color of the hide were a perfect match for local stone.
It traveled alone but we found evidence of a larger group nearby: overlapping tracks, multiple kills in a small area.
The blood attracted carrion birds within minutes. Whatever chemicals are in it, the scavengers could smell it from high up.
Its respiration was audible from a distance. A slow, wet, rhythmic sound. Like bellows being worked underwater.
The pelage changed density along the body. Thick and woolly at the core, sleek and flat on the limbs.
It used its head as a battering ram. The skull was reinforced: thick frontal bone, no horns necessary.
The creature shed teeth like a shark. Found rows of replacements behind the primary set. It would never go toothless.
Its eyeshine was blood red. Caught the torchlight from across the clearing and held it without blinking.
The footpads oozed a sticky residue that let it walk on vertical surfaces. Found prints on the interior walls of a ruin.
It ate carrion by preference but hunted live prey when desperate. The shift in behavior is what worried the scouts.
The spines were hollow and whistled in the wind. The creature sounded like a flute when it ran.
When threatened, it curled into a tight ball. The outer shell was impenetrable: arrows, blades, nothing got through.
The lair stank of ammonia so strong it drove back the first scouting party before they reached the entrance.
It stalked the livestock but only took the weakest animals. Selective predation, like it was culling the herd.
The creature burrowed through hard-packed clay. Left tunnels wide enough for a person to crawl through.
Its throat sac inflated when it called. Bright orange, visible from a considerable distance. Display or communication.
The wings generated a deep hum in flight. Not a buzz: a resonant drone you could hear before you saw the creature.
It played with its food. Caught, released, caught again. Not hunger: practice, or maybe something worse.
The creature was covered in ticks the size of a thumbnail. Whatever sustains the host apparently sustains a whole ecosystem.
Its tracks showed it had been circling the settlement for days. Never closer than the treeline, never farther than the ridge.
The beak could crush bone. The smithy found a deer skull in the field, punctured clean through the cranium.
It regurgitated pellets containing metal objects. Nails, buckles, a coin. It had been eating through someone's equipment.
The molt left behind a complete exoskeleton. Intact, hollow, brittle. An exact replica of the living creature.
Its tracks were erratic near the den: tight circles, sudden direction changes. Disoriented or distressed.
Found claw sharpening marks on a granite outcrop. Deep parallel grooves, polished smooth from repeated use.
The creature dragged vegetation into its burrow. Lined the interior with dry leaves and bark. Nesting behavior.
One of the hunters described its movement as rowing through the air. Not flapping: long, sweeping strokes.
It tested the fence. Pressed against each section methodically, found the weakest point, and went through.
The creature's eyes tracked independently. One watched the hunter while the other scanned the treeline.
An old trapper recognized the droppings. Said his father trapped something similar two generations back. Thought they were gone.
It responded to whistling. Not aggressively: it stopped, oriented toward the sound, and listened. Then moved on.
The impact crater where it landed was three handspans deep. It drops from above and uses weight to stun prey.
The creature was missing a limb and had adapted its gait. Still fast. Still dangerous. Maybe more so.
The fin was rigid, not cartilaginous. Bone-supported. That means it is not a fish in any familiar sense.
It left scratch marks at exactly the same height on every tree. Marking its reach, establishing dominance.
Its body temperature was perceptibly warm. You could feel the heat radiating from two paces away.
When it fled, it released a cloud of fine particles. Choking, irritating. Covered its retreat.
The young clung to the underside of the adult. Attached by small hooks on their forelimbs. A dozen of them, at least.
It swallowed stones deliberately. Gizzard stones, probably, for grinding food. Found a pile of polished pebbles near the den.
Its vocalizations changed depending on the audience. Deeper around intruders, higher and softer around its young.
The wounds it inflicted were ragged. Not clean cuts: tearing damage, like being caught in machinery.
Phosphorescent mucus trailed behind it at night. You could follow the path it took hours later by the faint glow.
The creature appeared to be mapping. It visited the same locations in the same order each night, pausing at each one.
Two forward-facing eyes and a cluster of smaller ones along the jawline. Peripheral vision must be nearly complete.
It used the river current. Entered upstream and let itself be carried to the hunting ground. Conserving energy.
The creature's defense was noise. A shriek that made your inner ear ache and your vision blur.
The scat contained fragments of worked stone. It had been chewing on the ruins.
Its gait left a distinctive pattern: two-by-two, like a rabbit's hop, but scaled up enormously.
It hung inverted from the cave ceiling, motionless, for the entire day. Only dropped at nightfall.
The creature avoided metal. Steered around iron fittings, refused to enter structures with nails. Some kind of sensitivity.
The scent glands were behind the ears. You could see the oily patch where the fur was flattened and dark.
It followed the patrol at a fixed distance. Matched their speed exactly. When they stopped, it stopped.
The creature's breath condensed into a visible plume even in warm weather. Something about its internal temperature.
Found scratch marks inside a hollow log. The creature had been sheltering in tight spaces, not denning in the open.
It targeted the eyes of larger prey. Every carcass we found had the same damage pattern: eyes gone, throat opened.
The tentacles were retractable. Flush against the body when moving, extended only when feeding or fighting.
Its coloring served as a warning. Bright bands of orange and black. In nature, that pattern means one thing.
The creature could dislocate its own shoulders to squeeze through narrow gaps. Watched it compress and push through a crack.
A thin membrane connected the forelimbs to the torso. Not wings: a gliding surface. It launched from high points.
The thing communicated by posture. Flattened meant submissive. Arched back meant aggressive. The hunters learned fast.
It cached water. Found shallow depressions it had dug and lined with packed clay. Filled with rainwater.
The creature was drawn to sound. Specifically rhythmic sound. The mill wheel seemed to attract it.
The plating along its sides was layered over softer tissue. When struck, the plates absorbed the blow and the tissue flexed.
It hunted from concealment in shallow water. Only the nostrils and eye ridges above the surface. Perfect ambush predator.
The creature's droppings crystallized as they dried. Hard, angular, almost gemlike. Strange biology.
Its locomotion was serpentine despite having limbs. It wove between obstacles like it had no spine at all.
The feather crest rose when it was agitated. Lay flat when calm. Better threat indicator than any sound it made.
It stored food in tree forks, impaled on broken branches. Larder behavior, like a shrike but much larger prey.
When we found the den, the walls were polished smooth. Worn down by years of the creature rubbing against them.
It could rotate its head nearly full circle. The vertebral structure must be unlike anything conventional.
The secretion from its dorsal glands repelled insects. Even mosquitoes avoided the area around its lair.
It built elevated platforms from stacked debris. Observation posts, perhaps. It likes to survey from height.
The thing was patient beyond anything natural. Sat in the same position for two days, waiting for a kill that never came.
The creature's skin rippled independently of its muscles. Like something beneath the hide was shifting on its own.
It left tooth marks on the inside of its own den walls. Compulsive behavior, or perhaps the stone provides minerals.
The hind legs were disproportionately long. Built for a single explosive leap rather than sustained running.
When injured, it emitted a high-pitched whine that attracted others of its kind within minutes. A distress beacon.
The creature's fur was coated in a fine dust that irritated the skin on contact. The hunters wore wraps over their faces.
It constructed a false trail. Dragged a carcass in one direction, then doubled back and took the kill a different way.
The ridges along its spine were bony but flexible, folding flat against the back when it entered a confined space.
It consumed only the organs. Left the muscle and bone entirely intact. A selective feeder with specific nutritional needs.
The creature had a pouch. Marsupial-like, situated on the lower abdomen. The young peered out but never left it.
Its forelimbs ended in a single curved claw, thick as a finger, shaped like a sickle. One per hand, nothing else.
The tracks showed it had been running in formation with two others. Parallel lines, identical stride length.
It dragged its tail deliberately, leaving a smooth channel in the earth. Scent marking, possibly, or territory delineation.
The thing generated an electric discharge on contact. The trapper's hands went numb for an hour after handling the snare.
